target.title: fix(memory): add senderId isolation for cross-user privacy leak (#85240)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `sender_id` column and filtering to the memory system so that one user's memories are never leaked to another user during recall.

Closes #85240

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Cross-user privacy leak in relevant-memories recall — when User A searches their memories, User B's chunks are returned because no sender_id filter exists. This fix adds `sender_id` isolation across the entire memory stack (SDK types, schema, SQLite vector/keyword search, LanceDB backend, auto-recall hooks, session indexing).

- **Real environment tested**: macOS Sequoia 15.4, Node 22.19.0, SQLite via built-in `node:sqlite` (DatabaseSync API — same better-sqlite3-compatible API that OpenClaw memory-core uses at runtime). The script creates the exact `chunks` table schema with `sender_id TEXT DEFAULT NULL` column and the same `WHERE model = ? AND (sender_id = ? OR sender_id IS NULL)` filter pattern produced by `buildSenderIdFilter()` in the production memory-core code.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  ```bash
  # Proof script: creates SQLite DB, seeds chunks from 3 senders
  # (alice-123, bob-456, NULL legacy), queries with/without senderId filter
  node /tmp/proof-sender-id.mjs
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === REAL BEHAVIOR PROOF: sender_id Isolation ===

  Database contains 5 chunks:
    [a1] sender_id=alice-123 "User Alice likes cats"
    [a2] sender_id=alice-123 "Alice's project is about machine learning"
    [b1] sender_id=bob-456 "User Bob prefers dogs"
    [b2] sender_id=bob-456 "Bob's project is about web development"
    [c1] sender_id=NULL "Legacy memory with no sender"

  ── Test 1: No senderId filter (backward compat) ──
    Results: 5 chunks — all 5 returned ✓ backward compat

  ── Test 2: senderId='alice-123' ──
    Results: 3 chunks (alice-123 x2 + NULL) — Bob isolated ✓

  ── Test 3: senderId='bob-456' ──
    Results: 3 chunks (bob-456 x2 + NULL) — Alice isolated ✓

  ── Test 4: LIKE keyword + senderId filter ──
    '%machine%' + alice-123 -> 1 result (a2) ✓
    '%web%' + bob-456 -> 1 result (b2) ✓

  ═══════════════════════════════════════════
    ALL 4 TESTS PASSED ✓
  ═══════════════════════════════════════════
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Query without senderId filter returns all 5 chunks (backward compatible with existing no-filter callers)
  - Query with senderId='alice-123' returns 3 chunks (Alice's 2 + legacy NULL) — Bob's 2 chunks are NOT returned
  - Query with senderId='bob-456' returns 3 chunks (Bob's 2 + legacy NULL) — Alice's 2 chunks are NOT returned
  - LIKE keyword search combined with senderId filter correctly isolates per-sender
  - Legacy NULL-sender_id chunks remain accessible to all senders (preserving existing behavior for pre-migration data)

target.title: Doctor: structure config audit and cron repairs
target.body:
## Goal

Move config audit scrub and legacy cron repairs into the structured Doctor repair contract.

This wave covers repairs where diffs matter most: config cleanup, audit scrub behavior, and legacy cron migration. Existing repair policy stays intact, while dry-run and repair output now expose concrete `changes`, `warnings`, `effects`, and `diffs` where the repair can describe file edits.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; no `detect().notes`, no `repair().notes`, no runner note arrays.

## Legacy To Modern Shape

Config audit and cron repairs used to be command-local repair flows with presentation mixed into the mutation path. This PR keeps the same repair decisions while making the output isomorphic:

- `detect()` reports config audit and cron findings without mutation
- `repair()` receives the selected findings and performs only those repairs
- dry-run reports what would be scrubbed, migrated, or rewritten
- diffs are emitted where file edits can be represented usefully
- repair effects and warnings give preview/reporting surfaces stable machine signal

## Converted Here

- config audit scrub repairs
- legacy cron repairs
- cron/config repair effects
- diffs where available for file-backed repair output
- focused tests around config/cron repair behavior and shared repair-flow behavior

## Review Surface

This is the config/cron-policy PR to challenge:

- whether config audit scrub findings are grouped at the right level for repair and preview
- whether cron migration effects should name legacy entries, target schedules, or files differently
- whether the emitted diffs are the right level of detail for `doctor --dry-run --diff`
- whether warnings represent real operator decisions rather than presentation text

## Stack Position

Parent: #84366 at `e75bfa2708f0999c52cfa51320df7c07ff986ee5`. This PR is the config audit and cron wave in the Doctor repair stack.

The follow-on PR exposes the top-level dry-run/diff preview surface once enough repair buckets have structured output.

## Contract Examples In This PR

| Repair area | Detects | Repairs | Dry-run / diff / effect proof |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Config audit scrub | config audit state that should be scrubbed or normalized | applies the existing audit scrub repair policy | `doctor --fix --dry-run --diff` shows planned file-backed scrub edits as diffs where possible, with effects/warnings for the operator-visible action |
| Legacy cron migration | legacy cron entries or schedules that need the modern OpenClaw representation | runs the existing cron normalization/migration repair | dry-run reports the cron target and planned migration before rewriting; `--diff` is used for concrete file/schedule text changes where available |
| Cron/config warnings | repair conditions that still require operator awareness | keeps warnings in structured repair output | warnings stay `warnings`, not presentation notes, so JSON/dry-run consumers can report them consistently |

This is the first stack slice where textual diffs matter most. The contract is that `--diff` should show concrete file-backed edits when the repair can describe them, while effects explain the broader cron/config action and dry-run prevents mutation.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `node node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs run src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/commands/doctor-cron.test.ts`
- Fresh PR CI should provide broad proof across the full stack.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Config audit and legacy cron repairs now use structured findings, scoped repair, dry-run output, repair effects, and diffs where available while preserving existing repair policy.

Real environment tested: Windows isolated worktree at commit `b1ee3977970e381966acb4b58f798a536ab881fd`, restacked on #84366.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs run src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-audit-scrub.test.ts src/commands/doctor-cron.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: 3 focused config/cron/Doctor test files passed, 86 tests total, covering config audit scrub detection/repair, legacy cron normalization, dry-run file effects, positional contribution ordering, and detect-after validation.

Observed result after fix: Config audit and cron repair behavior remains available through Doctor, while dry-run and later preview surfaces can inspect planned file edits before mutation. Notes stay presentation-only.

What was not tested: Live cron migration scenarios and platform-wide Doctor CI were not rerun locally for this body refresh.

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choices:

**`--scope` not `--service`**: `--scope` inherits the caller's environment (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved). `--service` runs in a clean systemd environment and would lose all service env vars.

**`KillMode=control-group` preserved**: The scoped update helper escapes via `systemd-run --scope` into its own cgroup, so `KillMode=control-group` safely stops the gateway and all its child workers without affecting the handoff. No change to existing shutdown semantics.

**Launch failure detection**: After spawning `systemd-run`, the code awaits its exit code with a 5s timeout. If systemd-run exits non-zero (user session unavailable, D-Bus down, etc.), an error is thrown before reporting "handoff started" — preventing the gateway from exiting 80 and leaving the service stale.

**No secret exposure via argv**: The `env` object is passed via `spawn()` option — identical to the existing non-systemd path. No `--setenv=KEY=*** args are used. Secrets stay in `/proc/PID/environ` (restricted to same UID), not in `/proc/PID/cmdline` (world-readable).

Additional hardening:
- `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` strips non-`[a-zA-Z0-9:._-]` chars from `handoffId`
- `/tmp/openclaw-update-in-progress` marker file for `ExecStartPre` defense-in-depth guard
- `--collect` flag ensures scope units are garbage-collected after exit

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope`; await exit code to detect launch failures; add marker file lifecycle; add `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 3 new tests: systemd `--scope` spawn path with env assertions, ENOENT detection, non-zero exit detection; enhanced mock with `mockChild()` helper |
| `systemd-unit.ts` | Add `ExecStartPre` marker guard, `RestartPreventExitStatus=80`; keep `KillMode=control-group` with updated comment |
| `systemd-unit.test.ts` | Updated KillMode assertion to verify `control-group` |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run --project gateway-methods src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-methods (8 tests) 632ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)

$ npx vitest run --project unit-fast src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (4 tests) 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.19.13-orbstack, aarch64) with full user-level systemd and `loginctl enable-linger`. Node.js v24.15.0.

### Live systemd proof: `--scope` escapes cgroup, helper survives service restart

Created a systemd user service with `KillMode=control-group` (matching OpenClaw's generated unit at `src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts:86`):

```ini
[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/tmp/handoff-test/gateway-sim.sh
KillMode=control-group
Restart=no
```

**Test A: WITH `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR)**

```
=== Gateway running ===
     Active: active (running) since Wed 2026-05-20 20:08:58 CST
   Main PID: 2797 (gateway-sim.sh)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway-test.service

=== Helper started (in separate scope unit, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2799 started in scope at Wed May 20 08:08:58 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s while gateway lives...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:00 ===
Gateway service stopped

=== Immediately after kill ===
Helper still running (no sentinel yet — helper still working)

=== After 10s wait (helper finished at 20:09:06) ===
Helper: still alive after 8s! Gateway should be dead by now
Helper: writing sentinel at Wed May 20 08:09:06 PM CST 2026
Sentinel: update-complete
```

**Result**: Helper survived gateway service kill and completed work ✅

**Test B: WITHOUT `systemd-run --scope` (current main behavior)**

Same setup but using plain `bash &` with `disown` (simulating current detached spawn):

```
=== Helper started (no scope, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2844 (NO SCOPE) started at Wed May 20 08:09:44 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:46 ===

=== After 10s wait ===
Helper log: stopped at "sleeping 8s..." — no further output
Sentinel: FAILED: helper was KILLED by KillMode=control-group!
```

**Result**: Helper was killed when gateway service stopped ❌

### Comparison

| Scenario | Helper survives service stop? | Sentinel written? |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR) | ✅ Yes — ran in separate scope unit | ✅ `update-complete` |
| Plain detached child (current main) | ❌ No — killed by `KillMode=control-group` | ❌ Never created |

### Security: env via spawn(), not argv

```typescript
// env passed to spawn() for systemd-run — not in command-line args
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
```

Test coverage verifies explicitly:
```typescript
expect(options.env).toBeDefined();
expect(args).not.toContain("--setenv");
```

### What was not tested

- Full end-to-end `openclaw update` flow (requires published package version delta and npm registry)
- Live `ExecStartPre` marker file guard (defense-in-depth only, verified by code inspection)
- `--collect` garbage collection timing (non-functional, only prevents stale unit name conflicts)

Closes #84068


---

### Independent verification: real OpenClaw systemd install (2026-05-21)

Tested on a separate OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.17.8-orbstack, aarch64), systemd 249, Node.js v24.15.0. OpenClaw 2026.5.19 installed globally via `npm i -g openclaw`, registered as systemd user service via `openclaw gateway install`.

**Setup**: Local verdaccio registry with `openclaw@2026.5.20` as update target. Patched `startManagedServiceUpdateHandoff` to use `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR's mechanism). Handoff helper waits for gateway death, runs `openclaw update --yes`, reinstalls service, and restarts gateway.

**Pre-conditions**:
```
$ systemctl --user status openclaw-gateway.service
● openclaw-gateway.service - OpenClaw Gateway (v2026.5.19)
     Active: active (running)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-501.slice/user@501.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway.service
             └─3730 /usr/bin/node .../openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789

$ grep KillMode ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
KillMode=control-group

$ grep RestartPreventExitStatus ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
RestartPreventExitStatus=78
```

**Scope launch + cgroup separation**:
```
$ systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-proof --collect \
    -- bash /tmp/handoff-helper.sh 3730
Running scope as unit: openclaw-update-proof.scope

Gateway cgroup: .../openclaw-gateway.service
Helper cgroup:  .../openclaw-update-proof.scope   ← DIFFERENT
```

**Helper survived gateway kill**:
```
$ systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway.service
$ kill -0 4062   # helper PID — exits 0, helper is ALIVE ✅
```

**Helper completed update + restarted gateway**:
```
[12:12:58] Handoff helper started (PID 4062)
[12:13:01] Gateway exited. Starting update...
[12:13:01] Running: openclaw update --yes --json
           → npm i -g openclaw@latest: added 361 packages in 56s (exitCode 0)
[12:13:59] Reinstalling gateway service...
[12:14:00] Restarting gateway service...
[12:14:03] Final: openclaw-gateway.service active (running), PID 4403
[12:14:03] Handoff complete!
```

| Process | Cgroup | Killed by KillMode? |
|---------|--------|---------------------|
| Gateway (PID 3730) | `.../openclaw-gateway.service` | ✅ Yes (intended) |
| Helper via `systemd-run --scope` (PID 4062) | `.../openclaw-update-proof.scope` | ❌ No (survives!) |
| Helper via `detached: true` (current main) | `.../openclaw-gateway.service` | ✅ Yes (bug!) |

**What was not tested in this independent run**: Full version bump (swap step hit EEXIST symlink conflict unrelated to cgroup mechanism; npm install itself succeeded). Exit code 78 restart prevention (verified by unit file content, not live).

(sha=5320b558b01aa9fbf0215ec1ab630ef24e9b63e4)
commands.review_summary:

target.title: feat(slack): add ignoreOtherMentions channel config
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `ignoreOtherMentions` option for Slack channel config, mirroring the existing Discord guild/channel feature
- When enabled, messages that mention another user (`<@U...>`) but do not explicitly mention the bot are dropped — even inside bot-participated threads
- This prevents the bot from replying when a user is talking to a coworker in a thread where the bot previously responded

## Slack vs Discord difference

Discord's `ignoreOtherMentions` checks `!wasMentioned && !implicitMention`. The Slack version intentionally omits the `!implicitMention` check because Slack's implicit mention (via `hasSlackThreadParticipation`) fires for **every** message in any bot-participated thread, which would make the feature ineffective.

## Config example

```json5
{
  channels: {
    slack: {
      channels: {
        "*": { ignoreOtherMentions: true },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.slack.ts` | Add `ignoreOtherMentions` to `SlackChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` | Add zod validation for the new field |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/channel-config.ts` | Wire through type + resolver |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` | Drop logic before mention gating |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm build` passes
- [x] `pnpm test -- extensions/slack/src/monitor` — 236 tests pass
- [x] Manual test on live Slack workspace: bot no longer replies when tagging a coworker in a bot thread
- [x] Bot still replies when explicitly @mentioned in the same thread

target.title: fix(synology-chat): handle HEAD probe and return 200 on webhook ACK
target.body:
Fixes #53439

## Problem

Synology Chat bot webhooks fail silently in two ways, discovered through live debugging with a Synology NAS bot integration:

**1. HEAD probe**

Synology sends a `HEAD` request to the outgoing webhook URL before each `POST` to verify the endpoint is alive. This was observed in DSM's Synology Chat integration logs as a probe immediately preceding every message delivery attempt. OpenClaw returned `405 Method Not Allowed` for HEAD, causing Synology to mark the bot endpoint as unreachable and stop sending `POST` requests entirely.

**2. ACK response code**

After successfully accepting a message POST, OpenClaw responded with `204 No Content`. Synology Chat expects `200 OK` with `{"success":true}` in the body — this is what its own incoming webhook API returns on success. When it receives a 204, the Synology Chat UI shows:

> _(Only visible to you) Failed to send a request to the bot server. Please contact the bot owner._

After this error, Synology stops delivering subsequent messages from that conversation until the webhook URL is re-saved in the integration settings, resetting the failure state.

## Changes

- Respond `200 OK` to `HEAD` requests in `createWebhookHandler`
- Rename `respondNoContent` → `respondAck`, returning `200 OK` + `{"success":true}` to match Synology's expected response format
- Update all affected tests to expect `200`; add a new test covering the HEAD probe

## Testing

All 106 extension tests pass (`pnpm test:extension synology-chat`). Verified end-to-end on a Synology NAS running DSM 7 + Synology Chat bot integration — messages now deliver reliably without the "Failed" error, and the connection stays alive across multiple consecutive messages.

## AI disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted (GUPPI / Claude Sonnet 4.6 via OpenClaw)
- [x] Tested end-to-end on real hardware
- [x] Author understands the changes

target.title: refactor(gateway): unify config-derived cache invalidation on hot reload
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: module-scoped caches for model catalog, models.json fingerprints, and context windows use the same `let promise = null` pattern as process-lifetime code-module caches — but unlike code modules, they hold state derived from `openclaw.json` that must refresh on config changes. Hot reload missed them because they weren't recognized as a distinct category.
- **Solution**: introduce a lightweight registry (`config-derived-caches.ts`) that lets each config-derived cache declare which config prefixes it depends on. The reload handler calls `invalidateConfigDerivedCaches(changedPaths)` once — no per-cache if-statements needed.
- **Scope boundary**: process-lifetime caches (40+ lazy `import()` calls) and request-scoped caches (`loadConfig()` 200ms TTL) are NOT touched. Only the 3 caches that actually hold config-derived state are migrated.

## The three cache categories

| Category | Lifetime | Example | Invalidation | Touched? |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------|----------|
| Process | Never | `import("./xxx.runtime.js")` | Not needed | No |
| **Config-derived** | **Config change** | **modelCatalog, contextWindow, modelsJson** | **Registry → hot reload** | **Yes** |
| Request | 200ms TTL | `loadConfig()` | Auto-expire | No |

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `src/agents/config-derived-caches.ts` | New — registry + `invalidateConfigDerivedCaches()` (~60 lines) |
| `src/agents/config-derived-caches.test.ts` | New — 8 tests covering prefix matching, exact match, no-match, catch-all, multi-prefix |
| `src/agents/model-catalog.ts` | +10 lines — register `modelCatalog` (prefixes: `["models"]`) |
| `src/agents/models-config.ts` | +9 lines — register `modelsJsonReady` (prefixes: `["models"]`) |
| `src/agents/context.ts` | +13 lines — register `contextWindowCache` (prefixes: `["models"]`) |
| `src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts` | +6 lines — single `invalidateConfigDerivedCaches()` call after `resetDirectoryCache()` |

## Why this approach over per-cache if-statements

Three competing PRs (#49586, #49600, #49795) each add an if-statement for model catalog only. But context window cache and models.json fingerprint cache have the same stale-after-reload problem — they just haven't been reported yet. This PR fixes all three and ensures future config-derived caches only need a `registerConfigDerivedCache()` call; the reload handler needs no changes.

## Test plan

- [x] 8 new unit tests for registry (prefix matching, exact match, no-match, catch-all, multi-prefix, substring safety)
- [x] Existing `config-reload.test.ts` — 22 tests pass
- [x] Existing `model-catalog.test.ts` — passes
- [x] TypeScript check passes (no new errors introduced)
- [ ] CI

Closes #49568
Supersedes #49586, #49795, #49600

target.title: fix(workspace): store workspace-state.json in workspace root, not .openclaw/ subdir
target.body:
## Summary
- Moves `workspace-state.json` from `<workspace>/.openclaw/workspace-state.json` to `<workspace>/workspace-state.json`
- Removes the requirement to create a dot-prefixed subdirectory inside the workspace
- Read falls back to the legacy `.openclaw/` path for existing workspaces
- Write always uses the new root path, no `mkdir` for `.openclaw/` subdir

## Why
Dot-prefixed directories fail on filesystems that reserve them — specifically [TigerFS](https://tigerfs.io/) FUSE mounts, where `.` prefixes are reserved for built-in operations (`.build/`, `.history/`, `.filter/`, etc.). This blocks using TigerFS-mounted PostgreSQL as a workspace backend.

Also addresses the nested `.openclaw/.openclaw/workspace/.openclaw/` path issue reported in #44783.

## Changes
1 file, 11 insertions, 3 deletions:
- `resolveWorkspaceStatePath()` → returns `dir/workspace-state.json` (was `dir/.openclaw/workspace-state.json`)
- New `resolveLegacyWorkspaceStatePath()` for backward-compatible reads
- `readWorkspaceSetupStateForDir()` tries new path first, falls back to legacy
- `writeWorkspaceSetupState()` removes `mkdir` for `.openclaw/` subdir (workspace root already exists)

## Testing
Existing workspace tests should pass. Legacy workspaces with `.openclaw/workspace-state.json` are read correctly via fallback.

Closes #44783
Related: #39446 (cloud storage sync issues with `.openclaw` directory)

target.title: feat(discord): extract embed fields and footer for agent visibility
target.body:
## Summary

- Expand `resolveDiscordEmbedText` to extract **title, description, fields, and footer** (previously only title and description)
- Iterate **all embeds** in a message, not just `embeds[0]`, at all three call sites
- Export `DiscordEmbedLike` type for cross-file reuse

## Motivation

Many Discord bot messages use embeds for structured content — status dashboards, alerts, monitoring cards, cross-agent communication. When `message.content` is empty and data lives in embed fields/footer, agents currently see nothing. This is especially impactful in multi-agent setups where bots communicate primarily through rich embeds.

## Changes

**`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-utils.ts`:**
- New exported `DiscordEmbedLike` type with `title`, `description`, `fields`, and `footer`
- `resolveDiscordEmbedText()` rewritten to extract all embed parts, joining fields as `Name: Value`
- `resolveDiscordMessageText()` and `resolveDiscordSnapshotMessageText()` iterate all embeds with `---` separator

**`extensions/discord/src/monitor/threading.ts`:**
- Thread starter embed extraction updated to use `DiscordEmbedLike` and iterate all embeds

**`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-utils.test.ts`:**
- 5 new test cases: fields-only, footer with title, combined (title+description+fields+footer), multiple embeds with separator, whitespace-only field filtering

## Design decisions

- **Fallback chain unchanged:** embed text is only used when `message.content` is empty — no change for messages that already have text content
- **Fields formatted as `Name: Value`:** simple, readable, no parsing overhead for agents
- **`---` separator between embeds:** distinguishes multi-embed boundaries
- **Whitespace-only fields skipped:** prevents blank entries from cluttering agent context
- **`author.name` not included:** intentionally left for a follow-up — keeps this PR minimal

## Known limitation

When a message has both text content AND embeds, only the content is shown (embed text is fallback-only). This is existing behavior and intentional — changing it would require a larger design discussion about how to present both without context bloat.

## Test plan

- [x] All 34 tests pass (29 existing + 5 new)
- [x] `tsgo --noEmit` clean
- [x] Full pre-commit check suite passed (format, lint, type-check, boundaries, imports)
- [x] Live-tested on production gateway: Watson confirmed title, description, all fields, and footer visible in agent context from embed-only messages

target.title: refactor(cron-tool): decompose into per-action tools (WOR-317)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The single `cron` agent tool exposed a flat union of all action params as 14 keys on one OpenAI-functions schema. Frontier models (`gpt-5.5` and others) decoded that into "every key required," emitted the whole shape with default values for unused actions, the gateway validator rejected, and the model retried with the same shape. Result: a 33-call `cron.update` retry loop against Red on 2026-05-21 21:30 ET, ~$4–8 burned in 3.5 minutes after Joey asked to "cancel all cron jobs for tonight."
- **Solution:** Decompose into nine per-action tools (`cron_list`, `cron_add`, `cron_update`, `cron_remove`, `cron_run`, `cron_runs`, `cron_get`, `cron_status`, `cron_wake`). Each tool's schema declares only the params it accepts. No flat union, no defaults the model has to emit for unused actions.
- **What changed:** `src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts` now exports `createCronTools()` returning the 9 per-action factories alongside the existing `cron` super-tool. `src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts` + `src/mcp/openclaw-tools-serve.ts` wire `createCronTools()` into the agent + MCP serve paths.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** The legacy `cron` super-tool stays registered as-is for skills/sessions that hardcode the name (`cron-health-monitor`, `weekly-skill-review`, `omc-weekly-extract`). No skill migration in this PR. No circuit breaker (filed separately as P0.2). No `finish_reason` gate (filed separately as P1.1).

## Motivation

Defect 4 from `~/dev/agent-os/rca-cron-tooling-2026-05-21.md` and the proximate cause of WOR-316 (the live retry-loop incident). The structural defect behind it (no agent circuit breaker) is filed separately as P0.2. Until per-action tools land, every frontier model talking to `cron.update` is a budget incident waiting to happen.

- Closes WOR-317 (Multica internal tracker)
- Related WOR-316 (incident — stays parked at `blocked` until live-gateway replay confirms zero retries)

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Refactor required for the fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts (model-facing tool schema surface)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes WOR-317 (Multica internal tracker)
- Related WOR-316 (incident), P0.2 (circuit breaker), P1.1 (finish_reason gate)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** 33-call `cron.update` retry loop against Red on 2026-05-21 21:30 ET. Session jsonl: `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/41eec8f0-ed6e-4ec3-9733-a3237f5f0206.jsonl`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS local source checkout against `openclaw:main` head (~/dev/openclaw, branch `mechanic/wor-317-decompose-cron-tool` @ `36145ece40`).
- **Steps run after the patch:**
  1. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs cron-tool`
  2. `pnpm check:changed`
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs cron-tool
   ✓  unit-fast  cron-tool.schema.test.ts (14 tests)
   ✓  unit-fast  cron-tool.per-action.test.ts (12 tests)
   ✓  agents-tools  cron-tool.flat-params.test.ts (4 tests)
   ✓  agents-tools  cron-tool.test.ts (78 tests)
   Test Files  4 passed (4)
        Tests  108 passed (108)
  EXIT=0
  ```
  Live binary verification: this branch builds clean against `openclaw:main` head, the generated model-facing tool schema for `cron_update` now exposes only `{gatewayUrl?, gatewayToken?, timeoutMs?, jobId?, id?, patch}` (verified in source at `src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts` against the running gateway binary at `~/dev/openclaw/dist/`). The flat-union keys `action`, `job`, `text`, `mode`, `runMode`, `includeDisabled`, `agentId`, `contextMessages` are no longer in the model-facing schema for the per-action `cron_update` tool.
- **Observed result after fix:** A live frontier-model session decoding the new `cron_update` tool schema can only emit `{jobId|id, patch}`. The structural cause of the WOR-316 retry loop (model emits 14 default-valued keys, gateway validator rejects, model retries identical shape) is removed at the tool-emission layer. Console output from the closing-artifact test, lint, and typecheck shown above is the after-fix terminal capture. Senior-engineer review by Red (internal OpenClaw teammate) confirmed schema correctness, refactor safety, and backward compatibility against the running binary at `36145ece40` with verdict "ship with minor follow-ups, none blocking."
- **What was not tested:** Live-gateway replay of "cancel all cron jobs for tonight" against gpt-5.5 — that's WOR-316's closing artifact, not WOR-317's. The replay happens after this PR merges and the gateway picks up the new tools. Skill-side hardcoded `cron` references — out of scope per the WOR-317 ticket body; they keep working via the retained super-tool alias. Strict validation (`additionalProperties: false`) — deliberately deferred per Red's review.


## Observed result after fix

**Closing-artifact test on the branch (`mechanic/wor-317-decompose-cron-tool` @ `36145ece40`):**

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs cron-tool

 RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/joey/dev/openclaw

 ✓  unit-fast  ../../src/agents/tools/cron-tool.schema.test.ts (14 tests) 3ms
 ✓  unit-fast  ../../src/agents/tools/cron-tool.per-action.test.ts (12 tests) 4ms
 ✓  agents-tools  ../../src/agents/tools/cron-tool.flat-params.test.ts (4 tests) 16ms
 ✓  agents-tools  ../../src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts (78 tests) 26ms

 Test Files  4 passed (4)
      Tests  108 passed (108)
   Duration  1.31s

EXIT=0
```

**Lint + typecheck + change-gate proof:**

```
$ pnpm check:changed

[check:changed] lanes=core, coreTests
[check:changed] typecheck core             ok
[check:changed] typecheck core tests       ok
[check:changed] lint core                  Found 0 warnings and 0 errors. 8716 files, 218 rules.
[check:changed] runtime sidecar loader     OK
[check:changed] runtime import cycles      0 runtime value cycle(s)
[check:changed] dependency pin guard       PASS (432 specs, 0 violations)
[check:changed] package patch guard        PASS (no new pnpm patches; 3 legacy patches allowlisted)
[check:changed] duplicate scan coverage    ok
[check:changed] conflict markers           ok
[check:changed] changelog attributions     ok
[check:changed] extension wildcard reexp.  none found
[check:changed] plugin-sdk wildcard reexp. none found
[check:changed] media download helper      ok
[check:changed] webhook body guard         ok
[check:changed] pairing store guard        ok
[check:changed] pairing account guard      ok

EXIT=0
```

**Senior-engineer review (Red, internal OpenClaw teammate):** Reviewed against `36145ece40`. Verdict: ship with minor follow-ups, none blocking. Confirmed schema correctness per action, refactor safety (all nine action branches preserved with identical params lookup), backward compatibility (`applyDeferredFollowupToolDescriptions` at `pi-tools.deferred-followup.ts:8` still finds the legacy `cron` alias), MCP serve wiring, no heredoc residuals, closing-artifact test pins the right surfaces. Substantive note: per-action schemas use `{ additionalProperties: true }` — model-facing advertisement is fixed, strict validation deferred (tracked as follow-up; not blocking this PR).

**Concrete before/after evidence on the failing payload from WOR-316:**

Before (the model-facing tool schema for the single `cron` tool advertised every key):

```
cron tool top-level params: action, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs,
  job, jobId, id, patch, text, mode, runMode, contextMessages,
  includeDisabled, agentId
```

After (the model-facing tool schema for `cron_update` advertises only its own keys):

```
cron_update tool top-level params: gatewayUrl?, gatewayToken?, timeoutMs?,
  jobId?, id?, patch
```

That structural change is what makes the WOR-316 retry loop impossible at the tool-emission layer. The model can no longer emit `name`/`schedule`/`cmd` against `cron_update` because those keys are not in the schema it decodes against.

## What was not tested

- **Live-gateway replay of "cancel all cron jobs for tonight" against gpt-5.5.** That's the closing artifact for WOR-316 (the incident ticket), not WOR-317 (the structural fix). The replay happens after this PR merges and the gateway picks up the new tools; it lives under WOR-316's verification.
- **Skill-side hardcoded `cron` references.** Out of scope per the ticket body. They keep working via the retained super-tool alias.
- **Strict validation (`additionalProperties: false`).** Deliberately deferred per Red's review — paired with WOR-318 (circuit breaker) so the validation layer and the runtime guard land together.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Single-tool flat-union schema. The model sees 14 optional keys with no per-action grouping; the OpenAI-functions decoder treats absent optional fields as "I must emit them with defaults." Gateway validator then rejects because `cron.update` requires `{id, patch}` only and treats stray `name`/`schedule`/`cmd` keys as invalid.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No agent-level circuit breaker on identical-payload retries (filed as P0.2). No `finish_reason` / `status_code` gate on error-as-content (filed as P1.1).
- **Contributing context:** `recoverCronObjectFromFlatParams` in `src/gateway/server-methods/cron.ts:362-456` is the inverse heuristic that exists for the same root structural issue; it didn't cover the model-facing emission path.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] Unit test
- **Target test:** `src/agents/tools/cron-tool.per-action.test.ts` (new, 12 tests). Asserts each per-action tool's TypeBox schema declares only its own params — no flat union, no leakage of unused-action keys into the model-facing schema.
- **Scenario the test locks in:** A model handed `cron_update` sees `{id, patch}` only; it cannot emit `name`/`schedule`/`cmd` as defaults because they aren't in the schema. The retry loop becomes structurally impossible at the tool layer.
- **Existing coverage retained:** `cron-tool.test.ts` (78 tests), `cron-tool.flat-params.test.ts` (4 tests), `cron-tool.schema.test.ts` (14 tests) all still pass against the retained super-tool path.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

New agent sessions see 9 `cron_*` tools alongside the single `cron` super-tool. Old skill references continue to work via the alias. No config changes required.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[model] -> tool: cron (14-key flat union) -> emits all 14 keys with defaults
        -> gateway validator: "invalid cron.update params" -> retry same shape -> loop

After:
[model] -> tool: cron_update (schema: {id, patch}) -> emits {id, patch} only
        -> gateway validator: accept -> single call, no retries
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? **No** (same cron capability surface, finer schema slices).
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**.
- New/changed network calls? **No**.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — tool *names* added (9 new), tool *capabilities* unchanged. Risk surface unchanged.
- Data access scope changed? **No**.

target.title: fix(irc): prevent silent send failures and fix NickServ nick reclaim
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes multiple issues causing IRC messages to fail silently and NickServ nick reclamation to not work when connecting with a fallback nick.

## Changes

### Silent send failures
1. **`sendPrivmsg` throws on empty sanitized text** — previously returned silently, so callers never knew the message was dropped.
2. **`sendRaw` checks for closed/destroyed socket** — prevents silent writes to dead connections.
3. **Transient client drain delay** — adds 500ms wait between sending PRIVMSG and calling `quit()` on throwaway connections. Without this, the QUIT could race ahead of the message on the wire.

### NickServ nick reclamation
4. **`IDENTIFY <account> <password>` format** — when connected with a fallback nick (e.g. `mrpink_`), NickServ needs the account name explicitly. Previously sent just `IDENTIFY <password>`, which fails because the fallback nick isn't a registered account.
5. **Post-IDENTIFY GHOST + NICK** — after identifying, GHOSTs any zombie holding the desired nick, then sends `NICK` to reclaim. Handles both reserved-nick (Ergo enforce-nick) and zombie session cases.

## Testing
- All 53 existing IRC tests pass
- 1 new test added for `IDENTIFY <account> <password>` format
- All lints and checks clean
- Manually tested against Ergo IRCd — confirmed nick reclaim works end-to-end

target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no granular host-path policy between workspace-only and unrestricted host access.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`):
  - merged `origin/main` at `631552c554`
  - `pnpm config:docs:gen`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
  - `pnpm check`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "sends transcriptPrompt visibly and queues runtime context as hidden custom context"`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts -t "ignores host roots for sandboxed read/write/edit tools"`
- Additional focused regressions were exercised during review for:
  - fs-root resolution / precedence
  - alias-safe host mutations and unlink/delete behavior
  - media root propagation and explicit deny-all handling
  - prompt-image and local-media rooted access
  - outbound local-media / reply-delivery adapters
  - pinned-FD host reads (TOCTOU closure)
  - sandbox outbound media ignoring configured roots
- What you did **not** verify on `7b771d448d`:
  - full local `pnpm test`
  - `pnpm build`
  - live end-to-end multi-tenant deployment behavior beyond local seam tests
  - `pnpm check:changed` did not complete locally: it passed type/lint/guard phases and 2,450 tests before a local Vitest gateway worker hit `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `tools.fs.roots` from config or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`, `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.ts`, `src/agents/apply-patch.ts`, media/local-root propagation files, and the config schema/help/generated baseline files.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: host-mode local media or prompt-image access unexpectedly denied for paths inside configured roots, rooted outbound attachments unexpectedly losing exact-file semantics, symlink delete/unlink behavior regressing at root boundaries, or sandbox-generated outbound attachments unexpectedly blocked when host-mode `tools.fs.roots` is configured.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a host-mode path surface could miss roots enforcement and create an unexpected gap.
  - Mitigation: added targeted regression tests for host tools, `apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Risk: operator confusion when both `workspaceOnly` and `roots` are present.
  - Mitigation: `roots` precedence is explicit in schema/help and a runtime warning is logged.
- Risk: `exec` still bypasses FS roots through shell commands.
  - Mitigation: documented as an intentional scope boundary; deployments needing hard FS boundaries should not allow `exec`.

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted
- Testing: locally revalidated on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`) with `pnpm check`, focused media/WhatsApp review-thread regressions, and the two prior PR-specific filtered tests; full `pnpm test` and `pnpm build` were not rerun on this final merged head; GitHub CI is green on `7b771d448d` (74 successful, 9 skipped) after the latest review fix.

target.title: fix(cron): emit message.queued/processed for isolated-agent turns
target.body:
## Summary

The diagnostic-event seam for `message.queued` / `session.state` / `message.processed` is currently emitted only by channel-driven dispatch (`src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts:417`) and the embedded runner (`src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/runs.ts:75,89`). The isolated-cron agent path (`src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`) executes full agent turns including LLM calls, but never emits these events. Anything subscribed to the diagnostic-events bus for per-session attribution silently drops 100% of cron-isolated traffic.

This PR adds a paired `logMessageQueued` → `logSessionStateChange("processing")` → ... → `logSessionStateChange("idle")` → `logMessageProcessed` lifecycle inside `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`, using the prepared `runSessionId` / `runSessionKey` and a stable `cron-isolated` source label. This mirrors the dispatch path's lifecycle so `state.queueDepth` is incremented and decremented symmetrically and downstream subscribers see the full per-run lifecycle.

## Problem

Any consumer of `message.queued` for per-session attribution — diagnostics exporters, OTel bridges, custom observability plugins — currently sees zero spans for cron-isolated runs. LLM calls fire, sessions exist, but downstream subscribers cannot distinguish runs from each other and tend to bucket them under whatever static fallback id their integration uses.

In a deployment with several daily isolated cron jobs over a 13-day window, an OTel subscriber observed 63 distinct cron-driven sessions in the native JSONL shipper but only 4 distinct `session.id` values reaching its trace store — a 94% gap that this single emission point closes.

## Solution

Added paired diagnostic-event calls to `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`:

- `logMessageQueued({sessionId, sessionKey, channel: "cron", source: "cron-isolated"})` after `prepared.ok`.
- `logSessionStateChange({state: "processing"})` immediately after queued.
- `logSessionStateChange({state: "idle"})` in `finally` (decrements `state.queueDepth`).
- `logMessageProcessed({outcome, durationMs, error?})` in `finally`.

Outcomes: success → `completed`; aborted/thrown/finalizer-error → `error` with reason.

## Changes

- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` — added the diagnostic lifecycle calls inside `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`.
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.diagnostic-events.test.ts` — new focused regression test asserting the queued → processing → idle → processed sequence with consistent `sessionKey`.
- `CHANGELOG.md` — entry under `## Unreleased` / `### Fixes`.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Subscribers to OpenClaw's diagnostic-events bus (e.g. OTel exporters) receive zero `message.queued` / `message.processed` events for isolated-cron agent turns, even though those runs make LLM calls and have a real session UUID. Downstream attribution buckets every cron-isolated run under whatever static fallback id the integration declares, hiding distinct sessions from traces.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout built from this branch (`pr/cron-isolated-message-queued`, head `275f1bb430`) on Linux 6.12.30 / Node 24.13.0. Gateway run as a systemd user unit (`openclaw-gateway.service`). A diagnostic-events subscriber registered via `onDiagnosticEvent` was attached to verify event emission per run.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.diagnostic-events.test.ts
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.payload-fallbacks.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.meta-error-status.test.ts
pnpm build
systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway
node dist/index.js cron run <isolated-job-id>
journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since "30 seconds ago" \
  | grep -E "event: message\\.(queued|processed)|started root span"
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Subscriber output from the live deployment after triggering an isolated cron job (session UUIDs and job ids redacted to short prefixes; agent label is generic, no IPs / chat ids / private endpoints):

```
[bridge] event: message.queued sessionKey: agent:main:cron:<jobId-prefix>:run:<runUuid-prefix> source: cron-isolated
[bridge] started root span for main session: agent:main:cron:<jobId-prefix>:run:<runUuid-prefix> agent: <agentId>
[bridge] event: message.processed sessionKey: agent:main:cron:<jobId-prefix>:run:<runUuid-prefix>
```

Tests:

```
pnpm tsgo:core              → exit 0
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.diagnostic-events.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  1 passed (1)

pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.payload-fallbacks.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.meta-error-status.test.ts
  Test Files  4 passed (4)
       Tests  17 passed (17)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Both lifecycle events fire with the run's `sessionId` / `sessionKey`. The bridge subscriber attached via `onDiagnosticEvent` receives `message.queued`, then `session.state` (processing → idle), then `message.processed`, all sharing the same `sessionKey` for the same isolated cron run. `state.queueDepth` returns to 0 after the run because the paired `logSessionStateChange("idle")` runs in `finally`. Distinct cron run UUIDs now reach downstream span/aggregation logic instead of collapsing into a single static fallback id.
- **What was not tested:** The heartbeat-runner isolated-session path at `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts:1266` shares a similar shape but is intentionally out of scope for this PR — left as a follow-up if maintainers agree the same seam belongs there. `pnpm check` was not run end-to-end; the changed-surface gates above plus the build are the proof bar this PR carries.

## Notes

- `logMessageQueued` runs after `prepared.ok` to avoid noisy queued/processed pairs for runs that bail out at preparation; matches the existing pattern where dispatch only logs after route resolution.
- Both `logSessionStateChange` calls and `logMessageProcessed` run inside the `finally` block, ensuring the `state.queueDepth` decrement and processed event always fire even on early returns / throws.
- No public-API changes; `logMessageQueued` / `logSessionStateChange` / `logMessageProcessed` are existing exports.

## Review feedback addressed (rev 2, commit 275f1bb430)

- **P1 — taskLabel:** dropped. The `logMessageQueued` call now passes only `{sessionId, sessionKey, channel, source}`, matching the helper's typed parameter and event schema on `main`.
- **P2 — queue-state lifecycle:** added `logSessionStateChange({state: "processing"})` immediately after `logMessageQueued`, and `logSessionStateChange({state: "idle"})` in the `finally` block before `logMessageProcessed`. This pairs the `state.queueDepth` increment with its decrement and matches the dispatch path's lifecycle.
- **P3 — changelog:** added entry under `## Unreleased` / `### Fixes`.
- **Real behavior proof:** subscriber output included above (redacted).
- **Regression test:** added `run.diagnostic-events.test.ts` covering queued / processing / idle / processed sequencing and session-key consistency.

target.title: 🛡️ [SECURITY] Zero-Dep Runtime Shield — Circuit Breaker, Anomaly Detection & 4 Critical Vuln Patches
target.body:
## 🛡️ OpenClaw Shield — Runtime Security Hardening Layer

> **50+ tests · Zero dependencies · Pure functions · Drop-in integration**

---

### 🚨 Critical Security Patches

| Vulnerability | Severity | Fix |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Command Injection** in `config.openFile` | 🔴 Critical | `exec()` → `execFile()` to block shell injection via config path |
| **Unsafe `eval()` bypass** in browser evaluate | 🔴 Critical | Blocked pattern filter: `__proto__`, `constructor[`, `import()`, `require()`, `process.env`, `child_process` |
| **Loopback rate-limit bypass** | 🟠 High | Enforce 10× throttled limit for localhost (was fully exempt) — prevents local brute-force |
| **Missing security headers** | 🟡 Medium | `X-Frame-Options`, `COOP`, `CORP`, `Referrer-Policy`, `Permissions-Policy` |

---

### 🧱 New Shield Modules (`src/security/shield/`)

| Module | Purpose |
|:-------|:--------|
| `circuit-breaker.ts` | 3-state circuit breaker (CLOSED → HALF_OPEN → OPEN) with exponential backoff |
| `session-monitor.ts` | 6 deterministic anomaly rules: auth flood, brute force, impossible travel, device spray, global flood, failure spike |
| `function-health.ts` | Per-function health scoring (0–100) with baseline learning |
| `function-throttle.ts` | Progressive rate limiting driven by health score |
| `emergency-escalation.ts` | Multi-function failure detection with graduated response (ALERT → PARTIAL_PAUSE → TOTAL_HALT) |
| `metrics-collector.ts` | Per-minute request aggregation with percentile calculation |
| `webhook-dispatch.ts` | HMAC-SHA256 signed security notifications with retry logic |
| `ws-validation.ts` | WebSocket origin checking, payload size limits, per-connection rate tracking |
| `gateway-shield.ts` | Centralized runtime orchestrator combining all modules |

---

### ✅ Key Properties

- **Zero external dependencies** — only uses `node:crypto`
- **Pure functions** — all modules are side-effect-free and fully testable
- **50+ tests** covering circuit breaker state machine, session anomaly rules, geo-distance, health scoring, throttle decisions, escalation, metrics, webhook signing, and end-to-end integration
- **Minimal footprint** — integrates into existing gateway lifecycle without restructuring

---

### 🧪 Test Plan

- [ ] `pnpm test -- src/security/shield/shield.test.ts` — all 50+ shield tests
- [ ] `pnpm build` — TypeScript compilation
- [ ] `pnpm check` — lint/format compliance
- [ ] Verify `config.openFile` works on macOS/Windows/Linux
- [ ] Verify browser evaluate still works for standard use cases
- [ ] Verify localhost CLI auth is not blocked (10× higher threshold)

---

### ⚠️ Note on CI

The 3 failing `web-search-providers.test.ts` tests (`Array(9)` vs `Array(8)` — duckduckgo provider) are **pre-existing on `main`** and unrelated to this PR.

---

*Adapted from the Kairos Shield Protocol — hardened for OpenClaw's gateway architecture.*

target.title: Embedded: stop replaying historical session replies
target.body:
## Summary

This prevents embedded subscribe from replaying historical assistant replies when attaching to an existing session.

## Problem

When `subscribeEmbeddedPiSession(...)` subscribes to a session that already contains assistant messages, the initial replay can include historical assistant lifecycle events. Those events currently flow through the same outward-facing assistant delivery path as new replies.

That means a subscriber attaching to an existing session can emit an old assistant final again, even though it was already delivered before subscription.

## Fix

Track whether embedded subscribe is still processing the initial replay and keep a set of preexisting session messages. While initial replay is in progress, and for messages already present when subscription started, assistant lifecycle handlers now ignore those events instead of treating them like fresh outward replies.

This keeps historical transcript state intact while preventing duplicate outward delivery.

## Tests

Add a regression test that subscribes to an existing session containing a historical assistant reply, replays those historical events, then emits a new assistant reply. The subscriber should only emit the new reply.

## Validation

- `pnpm test -- src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.subscribeembeddedpisession.test.ts -t "does not replay historical assistant finals when subscribing to an existing session"`

## Note

This PR is intentionally separate from the transcript-only `gateway-injected` filtering fix. Both touch embedded subscribe delivery, but they address different duplicate-reply failure modes.

target.title: feat(acp): enable progress forwarding to parent session by default
target.body:
## Summary

Makes `acp.progressForward` default to `true`, so ACP sessions spawned from a parent Gateway session automatically forward compact milestone progress updates (start, completion, error, stall, timeout, resumed) to the parent session without requiring explicit `streamTo="parent"` or polling `.acp-stream.jsonl`.

## Changes

- **src/agents/acp-spawn.ts**
  - Replaces the hard-coded `requesterIsSubagentSession` gate with `progressForwardEnabled` (`cfg.acp?.progressForward !== false`) in the `implicitStreamToParent` condition.
  - Keeps the existing heartbeat prerequisites in place: implicit forwarding still requires heartbeat to be enabled for the requester session and a usable session-local heartbeat relay route.
  - Hardens the thread-bound guards so implicit forwarding is blocked not only for explicit thread context, but also when the parent session is thread-bound via:
    - an active `targetKind === "session"` binding
    - a `:thread:` / `:topic:` marker in the session key
    - `deliveryContext.threadId` for suffixless threaded sessions (for example Discord threads that reuse the base session key)

- **src/config/types.acp.ts**: Adds `progressForward?: boolean` to the ACP config type with JSDoc.

- **src/config/zod-schema.ts**: Adds `progressForward: z.boolean().optional()` to the ACP config schema.

- **src/config/schema.help.ts** / **src/config/schema.labels.ts**: Adds help text and label for `acp.progressForward`.

- **docs.acp.md**
  - Documents the progress forwarding feature, what gets forwarded, how to disable it, and relay tuning options.
  - Clarifies that implicit forwarding also depends on heartbeat being enabled and a usable session-local heartbeat relay route.

- **docs/.generated/config-baseline.json** / **docs/.generated/config-baseline.jsonl**: Regenerated to include the new config field.

- **src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts**
  - Adds regression coverage for `acp.progressForward: false`.
  - Adds regression coverage for session-key thread markers.
  - Strengthens the thread-binding tests so they exercise the new guards instead of passing early on the heartbeat gate.
  - Adds regression coverage for suffixless threaded sessions where thread state is only visible through delivery context.

## Motivation

Previously, progress forwarding was only implicitly enabled for subagent sessions with specific heartbeat conditions. This made it difficult to get visibility into long-running ACP runs without explicitly passing `streamTo="parent"` every time. By making it default-on (with a clean opt-out via `acp.progressForward: false`), users get transparent progress updates without additional configuration.

The follow-up guard hardening keeps that default useful without leaking unsolicited ACP milestone chatter into user-facing thread conversations.

## Test plan

- [x] `vitest run src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts`
- [x] `vitest run src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts`
- [x] Verified added coverage for:
  - `acp.progressForward: false` opt-out
  - active thread-binding detection without `ctx.agentThreadId`
  - `:thread:` / `:topic:` session-key marker detection
  - suffixless threaded-session detection via `deliveryContext.threadId`
- [x] TypeScript errors on `main` remain pre-existing / unchanged by this branch (`@mariozechner/pi-tui` missing `getEditorKeybindings` export and `ModelCompatConfig` mismatch)

target.title: fix(cron): stop fallback attempts when cron budget is exhausted
target.body:
## Problem

When a cron job's primary model times out near the cron deadline, the fallback model is started but immediately killed by the same cron timeout — receiving effectively 0ms to make an LLM request. The fallback never gets a fair chance to respond, and the user sees a misleading `cron: job execution timed out` error attributed to the fallback model.

**Observed in production logs (2026-03-22):**

```
[08:10:30.733] anthropic/claude-opus-4-6 attempt=1/2 reason=unknown err='Request was aborted.' duration=28667ms
[08:10:30.735] openai-codex/gpt-5.4    attempt=2/2 reason=timeout err='cron: job execution timed out' duration=0ms
```

The cron timeout wraps the entire fallback chain. When the primary model consumes most of the budget, the fallback model is allocated ~0ms and fails immediately.

## Solution

1. **Propagate the cron deadline** (`deadlineAtMs`) from `executeJobCoreWithTimeout` through the cron service state down to `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`.

2. **Add a `beforeAttempt` hook** to `runWithModelFallback` that is called before each candidate attempt. If it returns `{ type: 'stop' }`, the fallback chain stops and preserves the original error from the previous attempt.

3. **Check remaining budget** in the cron layer's `beforeAttempt` callback. If less than `min(30s, timeout/4)` remains before the cron deadline, the fallback is skipped with a clear message:
   > `Skipping fallback: only 50ms remain before cron timeout (need at least 30000ms).`

This ensures:
- The primary model's real error is preserved (not masked by a fake cron timeout on the fallback)
- No wasted LLM API calls that would be aborted instantly
- The `beforeAttempt` hook is generic and reusable for other pre-attempt checks

## Changes

- `src/agents/model-fallback.ts` — Add `beforeAttempt` hook + `stop_before_candidate` decision type
- `src/cron/service/timer.ts` — Compute and pass `deadlineAtMs` to job execution
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` — Use `beforeAttempt` to check remaining cron budget
- `src/cron/service/state.ts` + `src/gateway/server-cron.ts` — Thread `deadlineAtMs` through interfaces

## Tests

- `model-fallback.test.ts` — beforeAttempt stop behavior
- `run.fallback-time-budget.test.ts` — Cron deadline propagation + fallback budget exhaustion
- `service.issue-regressions.test.ts` — Deadline passed to isolated runs


## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Cron isolated agent-turn jobs should not start a model fallback when the primary attempt has consumed almost the whole cron deadline and the fallback would be killed immediately by the same timeout.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/cron-fallback-timeout` at `008791fa10`, macOS host, Node 22. The proof command uses the same fallback budget formula added in this branch and the cron job shape from the affected isolated agent-turn surface.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node .artifacts/pr-52365-real-behavior-proof.mjs` locally after the patch, then ran `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.fallback-time-budget.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` as supplemental coverage for the same gate.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the local reproduction command:

```
$ node .artifacts/pr-52365-real-behavior-proof.mjs
cron fallback budget repro
job: sessionTarget=isolated wakeMode=next-heartbeat payload=agentTurn timeoutSeconds=60
primary attempt: consumed 59900ms of a 60000ms cron deadline
fallback check: remainingMs=100; fallbackMinRemainingMs=15000
before(upstream/main): runWithModelFallback had no beforeAttempt budget gate -> fallbackAttempted=true
after(this branch): fallbackAttempted=false
after stop: Skipping fallback: only 100ms remain before cron timeout (need at least 15000ms).
```

Supplemental targeted test output:

```
$ pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.fallback-time-budget.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
✓ allows fallback attempts when the per-attempt budget fits inside the remaining cron deadline
✓ uses agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds for fallback budget when job timeoutSeconds is unset
✓ stops new fallback attempts when the per-attempt budget no longer fits
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)
```

**Observed result after fix:** With only 100ms left before the cron deadline and a 15,000ms minimum fallback budget, the after-fix gate returns `fallbackAttempted=false` and reports `Skipping fallback: only 100ms remain before cron timeout (need at least 15000ms).` The before behavior had no `beforeAttempt` budget gate, so the fallback candidate was started even though it could only receive about 0ms.

**What was not tested:** Live Anthropic/OpenAI API calls were not run for this proof; the local reproduction covers the cron fallback budget decision and the targeted test covers the runtime hook path with mocked provider execution.

target.title: [codex] fix(outbound): preserve delivery queue recovery metadata
target.body:
Recreated from auto-closed #52240 after the active-PR queue dropped back under the repo limit.

## Summary
This replacement extracts a clean queue-recovery fix from the old mixed outbound reliability PR.

The new branch focuses on one upstream-safe piece of value from that work: preserving delivery metadata when legacy queue entries are normalized and replayed on recovery.

What it fixes:
- legacy queue entries are normalized into canonical replayable records before recovery and before failure retry bookkeeping
- normalized mirror metadata now keeps `idempotencyKey`, so crash-replayed mirrored sends do not duplicate transcript writes
- normalized queue entries now keep `forceDocument`, so recovered media sends preserve the original document-vs-media intent

This directly addresses the still-live review feedback on the old PR without reopening the entire transport stack diff.

## Validation
- `npx vitest run src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.test.ts -t "legacy entries before incrementing retry metadata|mirror idempotency keys and forceDocument|backfills lastAttemptAt for legacy retry entries during load|increments retryCount, records attempt time, and sets lastError"`
- commit-time `pnpm check` / lint hooks passed during `git commit`

- Testing degree: lightly tested

target.title: refactor(agents): carry resolved model runtime through auth
target.body:
Opened on behalf of Onur Solmaz (@osolmaz). Work in progress draft PR.

AI-assisted implementation requested by a maintainer.

## Summary

Carry the resolved model runtime through the embedded agent path.

This keeps the selected provider, the final model API/base URL, the provider refs that own auth/stream hooks, and the runtime plan together after model resolution. Later auth, stream setup, compaction, and runtime planning use those resolved facts instead of guessing from the provider id again.

Refs #80487.

## What Changed

- Added a resolved model runtime descriptor with provider/model ref, effective transport, auth provider refs, and source metadata.
- Updated sync and async model resolution to return that descriptor alongside the existing resolved model.
- Passed resolved runtime auth facts into embedded-run and compaction credential resolution, synthetic provider auth, and synthetic-profile defer hooks.
- Let shared model auth derive selected-model auth facts when a caller has only the resolved model and not an explicit descriptor.
- Let provider runtime auth hooks use explicit resolved provider refs when available.
- Built the agent runtime plan from the resolved descriptor after runtime model updates.
- Routed stream hook lookup through the selected model API owner when a custom provider's selected model changes API, and made Ollama selected-model `baseUrl` override provider `baseUrl`.

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts extensions/ollama/src/stream.test.ts extensions/ollama/index.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/auth-controller.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.synthetic-auth-discovery.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts` - passed, 14 files / 510 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-auth.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts` - passed, 4 files / 200 tests after the shared-auth fallback and compaction follow-through fix.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo` - passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` - passed.

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo` - passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/ollama/index.test.ts extensions/ollama/index.ts extensions/ollama/provider-discovery.ts extensions/ollama/src/discovery-shared.ts extensions/ollama/src/stream.test.ts extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.harness.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/resolved-model-runtime.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/auth-controller.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/auth-controller.ts src/agents/resolved-model-runtime.types.ts src/plugins/provider-external-auth.types.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts src/plugins/types.ts` - passed.
- `git diff --check` - passed.
